mnemonics.title: FILE LOCK TYPES
mnemonics.content: HARD LOCK: OS Enforced - CANNOT BE IGNORED / SOFT LOCK: Advisory - MAY BE IGNORED - HASH: Hard Always Stops Access; Soft Hopeful Suggestion

mnemonics.title: READERS-WRITERS PROBLEM
mnemonics.content: READERS CAN READ TOGETHER - WRITERS NEED SOLITUDE - REREAD: Read Easily, Exclusive Write Requires Exclusive

mnemonics.title: DEADLOCK CONDITIONS
mnemonics.content: MUTUAL EXCLUSION / HOLD AND WAIT / NO PREEMPTION / CIRCULAR WAIT - MOTH: Mutual Exclusion, One holds, Takes waiting, Circular chain

mnemonics.title: FILE SHARING RISKS
mnemonics.content: RACE CONDITIONS / DEADLOCKS / STARVATION / INCONSISTENCY - RIDS: Race condition, Inconsistent data, Deadlock, Starvation

acronyms.usage: Defines which users/groups can access files and what operations they can perform
Distributed file system protocol for sharing files over a network
Creates a private copy when a process modifies a shared file
Standard API that defines file locking functions like fcntl() and lockf()
Protocol used in distributed file systems for client-server communication

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a BOOK: Shared lock = multiple people reading the same book together; Exclusive lock = one person writing notes in the book, door locked, no one else allowed in
Two people holding different utensils, each waiting for the other to release their needed utensil - circular wait diagram with arrows pointing to each other's resources
Two runners crossing a finish line at the same time - whoever's timing is slightly ahead determines the winner unpredictably
Vertical timeline showing: Process A acquires lock -> Process B requests lock (blocked) -> Process A releases lock -> Process B acquires lock -> Process completes

analogies.analogy: Like a bathroom stall - when occupied (locked), others must wait outside until it's free. Soft lock is like a 'do not disturb' sign (can be ignored), hard lock is like a deadbolt (enforced).
A library reading room: multiple people can read (browse books) simultaneously, but when someone wants to write (add a new book), everyone else must leave so they have exclusive access.
A single pen in an office - only one person can use it at a time to sign documents. Others must wait until the pen is returned to the holder.
Like accessing files in a shared office drive - files appear on your computer but are actually stored on a central server that everyone accesses over the network.
Two friends wanting to shake hands but each is waiting for the other to extend their hand first - neither can proceed, both wait indefinitely.
